speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. I'm interviewing Mrs. Ruth Kurland of Chicago, Illinois. Tape number four.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you discovered that your brother-- your husband had a brother in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes,

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go about applying for papers to come to the United States? What was the procedure?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, we went to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we could get it faster. And we had cousins there. So we came to Canada. And in Canada--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: By boat-- Anna Salen. The boat was Anna Salen, I remember. We went from Austria to Germany. From Germany, we came to Halifax.

speaker: interviewer
text: What port did you leave from?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany? I don't remember. But I remember Halifax in Canada we came in the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was everybody a refugee on that boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. They have--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you received in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice. They were real nice. And we came to our cousins', and we stayed at the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in your party when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband and I and my daughter. She was three and a half years old. And we were in Canada. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then for a while--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak about your experiences when you were there with your relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the beginning. We didn't speak in the beginning. My husband was a cantor in Toronto, in the Romanian Synagogue. And, I remember, I took my daughter, and it was the High Holidays. And she was screaming, is Daddy there? Is Daddy singing?

speaker: interviewer
text: What language were you speaking with your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter used to speak German. When we came to Canada, she played with a little boy, and the boy spoke English. And she talked German with him. And he used to say, Tia, shut up. And she said, Ma, what is this? I said, don't worry. She learned so fast, English. I'm telling you, these children, they learn very fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke to her in Yiddish. But I suppose she doesn't know too good Yiddish, because she went to kindergarten there. And I gave her away to a woman who took care of her, and I went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Canada. For dresses. Sometimes I designed dresses there. And [NON-ENGLISH], my husband's brother, visited us from the United States, and they started making papers for us to go to the United States. And we came to Chicago, and we still live--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I still-- Oh, wait-- 1950-- I think 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. '51, you came to Canada. In '54?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 1954. Sure. And my husband, in the beginning, he worked for his cousin in the Illinois Condensing Company. He was a manager, and he used to [NON-ENGLISH] for the High Holidays in the [NON-ENGLISH] Synagogue. And then they listened to him. He was so good wonderful, that they took him to South Side Hebrew Congregation. And he was a full-time cantor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people want to know about your experiences during the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they want to know. But I didn't tell them much. I don't know why, in the beginning, we didn't talk about it. The only thing I talked is to my children. I want them to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your second child born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My second? In Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1957. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he went to a religious school. He graduated. And then he went to the Academy, graduated. He got a scholarship to Yeshiva University. And he went to law school there. He got his-- he went to bar in Chicago with one [NON-ENGLISH], one time. He was very good. My daughter went to the University of Illinois. She met her husband, the professor she married. She lives in Highland Park. And she has two beautiful children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, to your children, you did speak about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I talked to them. I talked to my children. And when my grandson, my daughter's, he went to the school, he came to me and said, Bubbe-- they call me Bubbe-- is it true that Hitler killed a million children? I said, yes-- because they talk to them in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what effect have your experiences had on the way you brought up your own children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came from a very nice, religious home. And we knew that the father's a father and the mother's a mother. We have to be nice. We have to listen to each other. And I taught my children the same thing. In our house, we never speak something-- dirty words, something, you know. When I started reading the books, here in the United States, my daughter was-- and I used to say to my daughter, look at the way they're writing the books. Such dirty words they're putting here. She said, Ma, they want to sell books. That's why they're writing like this. But, it was-- but in our house, we always-- we speak nice. And there was neighbors that were screaming or something. My house was always refined and nice. So is my daughter's home, and so is my son's home. They have-- they know-- When my daughter said that my mother, when I was five years, she brainwashed me that I have to go to college. When she was little, I used to say, you'll have to go to college. So, she said, she brainwashed me when I was five. She'll always remember me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, my daughter has a very nice home, a religious home. My grandson goes to the Academy, and the one is going to go to in [NON-ENGLISH], a Jewish school. And now he wants to go to Israel for a year. And then, when he returns, he'll go to college. Would you say that your experiences have affected your faith in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always believe in God, and I always have faith in God. Whatever has happened, I don't know why, because there's no answer. It's no answer for this, but I always have faith in God. I have faith. I came out alive. And, I don't know, sometimes I feel guilty that I'm just alive and everybody's gone. But maybe it's supposed to be like this. Maybe my children will bring something good in this world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you raise them in the Orthodox vein, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. My son is a wonderful person. He is-- I know in Israel, he's very famous. People, when they come to Jerusalem, and they don't know where to stay, and they say, where will you go? And they say, oh, we'll go to Stuart and Sheri's house, because it's always an open house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does he do in Israel, your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son is a-- works for [NON-ENGLISH]. It's a religious yeshiva. And he is a director-- and a special-- a special director that brings his students-- rabbinical students-- and they stay, and when they're finished, they became rabbis, he places them in the United States, in Africa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son is a rabbi. Sure. [NON-ENGLISH] in Jerusalem. From two of the best, highest rabbis. He [NON-ENGLISH]. And another thing, he used to teach the Michlalah, give lessons. But he didn't have a special-- in his class, whoever wants to come, from all school, they come, and he was teaching. But now, he opens a school, a girls' school for himself in Jerusalem. And we hope it will be successful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Kurland, why did you decide to tape your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: I decided to tape my story for the future generation. People should know what they did to us. People should know the way we suffer. And we had beautiful families. And we didn't do nothing to anybody. We were very nice to everybody. Why they did this to us?

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't understand. And I want people to know that we were always nice to people, and they paid us back with what? I don't know. So, I want for the future generation to know what we went through. And they should fight for their-- for their belief and for their religion. Thank you very much for allowing us to tape your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [INAUDIBLE] This shows that I was in Salzburg in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the DP Camp. So, this was from the Joint, from the Jew Joint, and the American people, they--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there, according to this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think a couple months, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES].

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a document that I was honest and a good citizen there before we left there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who released this document to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Austria. In Austria, they released this. Before you leave, they give you a paper that you are honest. Because, before you go to the United-- Canada, or wherever it is, you have to show--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] ?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's right. You have to show that you are a good citizen, a good-- and then they give you this.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll get into documents. Everything is to be documented, you know, as best--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This picture was taken in Rovne, when I was maybe 17. And I gave this to a boy, and I forgot about it. And being in [NON-ENGLISH], somebody came and said that they saw the picture, that a boy was looking for me. And I told him to come. He came to us, and I gave him the picture for myself with my husband, and I took this picture to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this that you got the picture back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married-- 19-- I don't know, around 1956, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, he had the picture from before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. For a long time. He had the picture for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other pictures of yourself--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture, you said it was your brother on the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my brother on the right. This is my brother in the right. And I got it when I was running away from home. I got it in a packet. And that's what I have the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean, it was just there in the breast pocket--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my breast pocket--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't realize you had it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know. No, I didn't know. I don't know when I put it in. I loved my brother. He was the oldest, you know, in our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's the oldest brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. It's older brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this taken in Rovne too?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Rovne.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he then? Do you know? On the occasion it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. He was born in 1919, so maybe he was 19 years old?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other pictures of any family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's it. You want to see more? No. This is the only thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians took him to the army. I never heard of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was conscripted by the Russians? And nobody ever heard from him again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never heard from him. They took-- this is my husband, Cantor Joseph I. Kurland. And the picture was taken in South Side Hebrew Congregation when he was a cantor there. The year-- I don't know. That's in the '50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother. He is a survivor. He lives in Israel. And this is his wife and his daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the brother that was with you when you ran away to Rovne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. He always said to me that I saved his life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he was younger, and I was, you know, holding him by the hand and doing everything for him. So, always, when he sees us, he says, you saved my life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my brother in Israel, and it was the first grandchild was born, so they're making the Bris, you know? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is in Israel. We visit Israel. So, there's my brother, and my husband, my son, and my brother's daughter. And I am there in the picture, and my daughter-in-law, and grandchildren. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, her husband, and my grandson. And it was taken maybe when he was-- I don't know. What year? He is 60 now, so, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 1982?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest grandson, William David.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Corey Scott. He's 10 years old. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My second grandchild, my daughter's. Yeah. This is my son, and Rabbi Shimon Kurland, his wife, Sheri, and his older daughter, Sara, and then Michal, and David, [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Triplets.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're triplets.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were visiting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were visiting in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here? They stayed--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, where do they live usually?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel, in Har Nof. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my granddaughter, Sara. She lives in Israel, and she was up for a visit here.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this year. Yeah. Son and my daughter-in-law, their triplets. There's [NON-ENGLISH] in the middle. And there's Michal and [NON-ENGLISH]. They live in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's this Purim.
